Title: Faculty offer-acceptance steps sometimes duplicate the central process
Description: Some faculties like Law and FEIT have their own offer-acceptance/orientation steps which overlap with central enrolment.

Title: Orientation content is mixed with administrative enrolment steps
Description: Orientation isn't just about events; faculties embed transactional enrolment mechanics into their orientation flows.

Title: Planning split across hub tool and faculty sample plans
Description: The hub holds the planning tools (My Course Planner, Handbook) while faculties hold the degree-specific sample plans — and the two don't link cleanly, so students bounce between them.

Title: FEIT re-hosts My Course Planner
Description: FEIT's top two course-planning pages are 'My Course Planner' and 'Faculty Course Planning Resources' — literally re-hosting the central tool to keep in sync.

Title: 46% of course planning is on school subdomains (MBS, Biomedical)
Description: Of 207 course-planning pages, 46% live on two school subdomains (Melbourne Business School and Biomedical Sciences) beneath their faculties — a level deeper than the faculty site.

Title: Faculties restate hub enrolment mechanics
Description: The hub owns the transactional act of enrolment, but faculties restate generic 'how to enrol/re-enrol' steps unnecessarily.

Title: Generic 'how to enrol' restatements add confusion
Description: When a faculty restates the central process, students don't know if the faculty page or the hub page is the definitive source of truth.

Title: Multiple explanations of the same process
Description: Enrolment steps explained differently on different sites.

Title: Faculty pages restate census dates and overloading rules
Description: Faculties re-explain rules for overloading and census dates, instead of pointing to the definitive policy.

Title: Timetabling explained multiple ways
Description: Different faculties write their own guides to MyTimetable, creating a maintenance burden and potential inconsistency.
